title: First Principles Thinking
tagline: Break it down to the irreducible basics, then rebuild.
description: Instead of reasoning by analogy ('we've always done it this way'), deconstruct any problem to its fundamental truths and reason up from there.
example: Elon Musk on battery packs: 'What are batteries made of? Cobalt, nickel, aluminium... What's the market price? $80/kWh. Can we assemble them ourselves? Yes: $29/kWh.' SpaceX was born the same way.
watchOut: Takes much more effort than analogy thinking — use it only for the decisions that really matter.

title: Occam's Razor
tagline: The simplest explanation is usually the right one.
description: When multiple explanations fit the facts, the one with the fewest assumptions is most likely correct. Don't multiply complexity beyond necessity.
example: Your phone doesn't turn on. Occam's Razor: it's probably dead (simple). Not: the SIM card malfunctioned and triggered a cascade failure (complex).
watchOut: Simple isn't always right. Don't oversimplify complex systems — use as a first filter, not final answer.

title: The Pareto Principle
tagline: 80% of results come from 20% of efforts.
description: In almost every system, a small number of inputs produce the majority of outputs. Find the 20% causing 80% of your results — then do more of that.
example: 20% of your clients produce 80% of your revenue. 20% of bugs cause 80% of crashes. 20% of exercises build 80% of your strength. Focus ruthlessly.
whoUses: Business strategists, Tim Ferriss, Warren Buffett
watchOut: The 80/20 split is illustrative, not exact. The principle is 'inputs are not evenly distributed' — not a mathematical law.

title: Inversion
tagline: Solve problems backwards — ask what you should avoid.
description: Instead of asking 'how do I succeed?' ask 'what would guarantee failure?' Identify and avoid the failure conditions. Often clearer than finding the path to success.
example: Charlie Munger: 'All I want to know is where I'm going to die, so I'll never go there.' To be a better parent, list everything a bad parent does. Then don't do those things.
watchOut: Inversion finds what to avoid, not what to do. Pair with positive goal-setting for a complete picture.

title: Dunning-Kruger Effect
tagline: The less you know, the more confident you feel.
description: Beginners overestimate their competence. Experts underestimate it. As knowledge increases, confidence first drops (you realise how much you don't know), then rises again.
example: After week 1 of learning guitar, you think you're almost there. After year 1, you realise how much you don't know. After year 5, you're genuinely good but still humble.
watchOut: Don't use it to dismiss confident people — sometimes they really are experts. Check their actual credentials.

title: Second-Order Thinking
tagline: Think about the consequences of the consequences.
description: First-order thinking: 'This decision has this result.' Second-order thinking: 'This decision causes this result — which then causes what?' Great decisions require thinking at least two levels deep.
example: Antibiotics kill bacteria. (1st order: good.) Overusing antibiotics kills all bacteria including good ones, breeds resistant strains. (2nd order: problem.)
whoUses: Howard Marks, Ray Dalio, chess players
watchOut: Second-order thinking can cause analysis paralysis. Know when good enough is good enough.

title: Hanlon's Razor
tagline: Never attribute to malice what stupidity can explain.
description: When someone wrongs you, assume incompetence, ignorance, or carelessness before assuming bad intent. Most bad things in the world are caused by error, not evil.
example: Your colleague didn't include you in the email thread. Hanlon's Razor: they forgot, not that they're plotting against you. This saves enormous emotional energy.
watchOut: Don't apply blindly to genuine patterns of harmful behaviour — some people do act with malice.

title: The Map Is Not the Territory
tagline: Your model of reality is not reality itself.
description: Every mental model, every belief system, every map is a simplification of the real world. Confusing your model with reality is the root of most intellectual errors.
example: Your business plan is a map. The market is the territory. No plan survives first contact with the market. Stay flexible — the territory always wins.
watchOut: This doesn't mean all models are equally wrong. Some maps are much better than others — update yours constantly.

title: Sunk Cost Fallacy
tagline: Past investment is not a reason to continue a bad decision.
description: The time, money, or effort already spent is gone — it shouldn't influence whether to continue. Only future costs and benefits should drive decisions.
example: You're 200 pages into a terrible book. The 200 pages are gone. The question is: will the next 100 pages be worth your time? If no, stop. Past pages don't count.
watchOut: Don't throw away genuinely valuable past investments too quickly. Distinguish sunk costs from ongoing obligations.

title: Circle of Competence
tagline: Know exactly what you know and don't know.
description: Everyone has a circle of things they genuinely understand. Operate inside it and your decisions will be good. Stray outside it without knowing and you'll fail.
example: Warren Buffett: 'I don't invest in tech.' He knows what he doesn't know. He stays in his circle and compounds returns for 60 years instead of getting distracted.
whoUses: Warren Buffett, Charlie Munger, Richard Feynman
watchOut: Circles can and should expand. The goal isn't to stay small — it's to know exactly where your edge ends.
